matches.match_rationale: Paper-based grade books and handwritten rubric scoresheets are non-digital inputs that must be digitized before any downstream automation can occur. OCR and handwriting recognition directly address the 'paper-based grade books at some sites' and the clerical aggregation burden described in the pain point. For a 500+ staff multi-site operator this is a well-scoped, technically mature capability.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A document-scanning pipeline that uses OCR/handwriting recognition to ingest paper grade books, converting them to structured records that feed directly into the SIS — eliminating manual re-entry at lagging campuses.

matches.match_rationale: The pain point explicitly calls out grades being re-entered into multiple systems and inconsistent grading scales across subjects and campuses. Data transformation addresses exactly this: mapping source records from one schema (per-campus gradebook format, inconsistent scale definitions) to a canonical target schema for the central SIS. This removes the clerical aggregation step without requiring a full system replacement.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI-assisted ETL layer that normalises grade records from per-campus exports (CSV, spreadsheet, or SIS API) into a unified schema, handling scale conversion (e.g. percentage to GPA) automatically and flagging unmappable entries for human review.

matches.match_rationale: A significant share of teacher grading time is spent writing qualitative feedback comments on assignments — a highly repetitive text production task well-suited to LLM generation conditioned on rubric criteria and student response. Standardising comment generation also directly addresses the 'no standardised rubrics' sub-problem by encoding rubric language into the generation prompt.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A teacher-facing grading assistant that, given a rubric and a student submission, drafts criterion-by-criterion feedback comments which the teacher reviews and approves in seconds — cutting per-paper time from minutes to under a minute.

matches.match_rationale: The full grading-to-record-keeping workflow spans multiple steps — collecting submissions, applying rubric scoring, writing comments, recording grades, and syncing to the SIS — which is exactly the multi-step orchestration profile for a task automation agent. The pain point's description of 8+ hours weekly and multi-system re-entry signals a workflow problem, not just a single-step AI assist.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An end-to-end grading workflow agent: ingests submitted assignments, scores against configured rubrics, generates feedback drafts, routes to teacher for sign-off, then posts approved grades to the SIS and gradebook automatically — reducing teacher involvement to review-and-approve only.

matches.match_rationale: Short-answer and essay rubric scoring is fundamentally a classification problem: assign a performance band (e.g. Exceeds / Meets / Approaching / Below) to a student text response based on rubric criteria. This is technically mature (fine-tuned LLM classifiers or zero-shot with structured prompts) and directly reduces the 8+ hours of manual scoring. Inconsistent grading across campuses can be addressed by standardising the classifier's label definitions.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A rubric-based auto-scorer for short-answer assessments that classifies student responses into performance levels, with confidence scores flagging borderline cases for teacher review — deployable per subject with a shared rubric configuration layer across campuses.
